samasa.vigraha: mānaś ca dṛk ca vicikitsā ca

analysis.note: One inflected enumerative dvandva; internal dṛś appears as dṛg before v by compound-internal sandhi. The feminine plural ending follows the final member vicikitsā-.

verification.detail: compound stem mānadṛgvicikitsā as Stri/Prathama/Bahu: pass; relation dvandva: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Catalogue nominative; an upakleśa in this context.
Catalogue nominative; final visarga is not printed before the following vowel in the verse sandhi.
Catalogue nominative; coalesces with following atha in the printed sandhi unit īrṣyātha.
Initial a is absorbed after final ā of īrṣyā in the printed īrṣyātha; parsed as the particle atha, not as -artha-.
Neuter nominative singular in the catalogue; the form is homonymous with accusative singular.

Instrumental singular governed by saha; because the commentary classifies this portion as upakleśa-material, the gloss is the moral-psychological sense ‘deceit,’ not metaphysical ‘illusion’ or ‘magic’.

justifications.decision: The compound is not a tatpuruṣa such as ‘doubt about a view of conceit’; Sthiramati treats māna, dṛg, and vicikitsā as separate items and then sums the preceding discussion as the six kleśas.
options_considered: tatpuruṣa: ‘doubt concerning the view of conceit’
justifications.chosen: mānadṛgvicikitsāḥ is an enumerative dvandva: ‘conceit, views, and doubt’.

evidence.quote: dṛg iti sāmānyanirdeśe 'pi kleśādhikārāt pañcaiva kleśātmikāḥ
evidence.translation: Although ‘dṛg’ is stated generally, because the topic is the afflictions, only the five that are afflictive in nature are meant.
‘Vicikitsā’: indecision regarding karma, its fruits, the truths, and the jewels.

evidence.quote: uktāḥ ṣaṭkleśās tadanantaroddiṣṭās tv idānīm upakleśā vaktavyā ity ata āha
evidence.translation: The six afflictions have been stated; now the minor afflictions mentioned immediately after them are to be explained.

justifications.decision: The English ‘conceit’ is chosen over generic ‘measure’, ‘opinion’, or ‘notion’ because the commentary defines māna by mental elevation and self-superiority.
justifications.chosen: māna = conceit/pride, specifically elevation of mind.

evidence.quote:
māno hi nāma sarva eva satkāyadṛṣṭisamāśrayeṇa pravartate
sa punaś
cittasyonnatilakṣaṇaḥ
tathā hy ātmātmīyabhāvaṃ skandheṣv adhyāropyāyam aham
idaṃ mamety ātmānaṃ tena tena viśeṣeṇonnamayati
anyebhyo 'dhikaṃ manyate
evidence.translation: All that is called māna proceeds dependent on personality-view. It is characterized by elevation of mind: having superimposed self and what belongs to self on the aggregates, one raises oneself in this or that respect, thinking ‘I am this; this is mine,’ and thinks oneself superior to others.

justifications.decision: Although the verse uses the general word dṛg, the commentary restricts it by the context of kleśa to the five kleśa-natured dṛṣṭis beginning with satkāyadṛṣṭi, explicitly excluding mundane right view and supramundane view.
justifications.chosen: dṛg = the five afflictive views, not ordinary seeing or right view.

evidence.quote:
dṛg iti sāmānyanirdeśe 'pi kleśādhikārāt pañcaiva kleśātmikāḥ
satkāyadṛṣṭyādikā dṛṣṭayaḥ saṃbadhyante
na laukikī samyagdṛṣṭir anāsravā vā
evidence.translation: Although ‘dṛg’ is stated generally, since the topic is afflictions, only the five views whose nature is afflictive, beginning with personality-view, are connected here—not mundane right view or the uncontaminated one.

justifications.decision: The commentary defines vicikitsā as vimati, indecision, specifically with respect to karma, fruits, truths, and jewels; hence not medical inquiry or mere curiosity.
justifications.chosen: vicikitsā = doubt/indecision regarding Buddhist doctrinal objects.

evidence.quote:
vicikitsā
karmaphalasatyaratneṣu vimatiḥ
vividhā matir vimatiḥ syān na
syād iti
prajñātaś ceyaṃ jātyantaram evoktā
evidence.translation: ‘Vicikitsā’: indecision regarding karma, its fruits, the truths, and the jewels. ‘Indecision’ is divided thinking—‘it may be, it may not be’; and this has been stated as a distinct kind from prajñā.

justifications.decision: The bhāṣya line quotes īrṣyātha without r; metrically and syntactically this is the nominative īrṣyā followed by the particle atha introducing the next item.
justifications.chosen: īrṣyātha is parsed as īrṣyā + atha, ‘envy; then …’, not as īrṣyārtha-.

evidence.quote: mrakṣaḥ pradāśa īrṣyātha mātsaryaṃ saha māyayā
evidence.translation: Concealment, spite, envy—then stinginess together with deceit.

justifications.decision: The commentary introduces the quoted sequence as the upakleśas now to be explained. Therefore mātsaryam, though identical in form to an accusative, is a neuter nominative in the list, and saha māyayā is comitative: ‘together with deceit’.
options_considered: a nominative list of upakleśas with comitative saha
mātsaryam as an accusative object of an implicit verb
māyayā as an instrumental of means, ‘by deception’
justifications.chosen: mrakṣaḥ, pradāśaḥ, īrṣyā, and mātsaryam are nominative catalogue items; māyayā is instrumental governed by saha.

evidence.quote: uktāḥ ṣaṭkleśās tadanantaroddiṣṭās tv idānīm upakleśā vaktavyā ity ata āha
evidence.translation: The six afflictions have been stated; now the minor afflictions mentioned immediately after them are to be explained.

evidence.quote: mrakṣaḥ pradāśa īrṣyātha mātsaryaṃ saha māyayā
evidence.translation: Concealment, spite, envy—then stinginess together with deceit.

one_shot_delta: A commentary-blind translation might take mānadṛgvicikitsāḥ as a semantic compound such as ‘doubt about the view of conceit’; Sthiramati instead defines māna, dṛg, and vicikitsā separately and counts them among the six kleśas.
It might render dṛg as ordinary ‘seeing’ or even as right view; the commentary restricts it to the five afflictive views and explicitly excludes laukikī samyagdṛṣṭi and anāsravā dṛṣṭi.
It might translate māna by a dictionary sense such as ‘opinion’ or ‘measure’; Sthiramati’s gloss requires ‘conceit’, the elevation of mind that thinks itself superior.
It might read īrṣyātha as a corrupted or variant īrṣyārtha; the bhāṣya quotation supports īrṣyā + atha, ‘envy; then …’.
It might take mātsaryaṃ as an accusative or māyayā as ‘by illusion’; the commentary’s catalogue frame makes mātsaryaṃ a neuter nominative upakleśa and saha māyayā a comitative phrase.
analyzer_disagreements: ByT5 represents māna and dṛś as compound components and vicikitsāḥ as the inflected item; I record the checkable word as the whole inflected dvandva stem mānadṛgvicikitsā- in Prathama Bahu Stri, since the commentary treats māna, dṛg, and vicikitsā separately and then counts the completed set as the six kleśas (trbh 715, 752, 767, 772).

ByT5’s SNNe tag for mātsaryam is formally ambiguous between nominative and accusative neuter singular; I specify Prathama because the verse is a nominative catalogue introduced by the commentary as upakleśāḥ vaktavyāḥ and then quoted (trbh 772, 774).
open_questions: The supplied commentary span stops before any detailed in-span definitions of mrakṣa, pradāśa, īrṣyā, mātsarya, and māyā; their English glosses here are the standard Buddhist-technical lexical glosses constrained by trbh 772’s classification of this sequence as upakleśas.
